title: A Creative Writing Workshop
summary: A creative writing tutor and a student discuss the craft of fiction — exploring voice, point of view, narrative distance, show-don't-tell, and the ethics of writing characters whose experience differs from the author's own.

body.speaker: Tutor
body.text: I've read your new draft, and there's something genuinely promising in it. But I want to push back on a choice you've made almost invisibly — the narrative voice. Who is telling this story, and how much do they know?

body.speaker: Student
body.text: I assumed a third-person omniscient narrator — someone who can see everything, knows what everyone's thinking. Isn't that just… the default?

body.speaker: Tutor
body.text: That's exactly the assumption I'd challenge. Omniscience is not a neutral default — it's a stance that carries its own ideological weight. When you know everything about every character simultaneously, you're enacting a kind of god-like authority over them. That can work, but it needs to be a deliberate choice, not a fallback.

body.speaker: Student
body.text: So what's the alternative? I don't want to be trapped inside one character's head the whole time.

body.speaker: Tutor
body.text: You don't have to be. Consider close third-person — you're technically outside the character, in third-person grammar, but your access to their interiority is deep and selective. The restriction is the point: what the narrator doesn't know creates as much meaning as what they do.

body.speaker: Student
body.text: That reminds me of something Henry James wrote about the 'central consciousness' — the idea that filtering experience through a limited perspective actually creates more intensity than total access would.

body.speaker: Tutor
body.text: Precisely. James understood that limitation generates pressure. When you can only perceive through one consciousness, the gaps and uncertainties become structurally significant. The reader does interpretive work the omniscient narrator would otherwise do for them, and that engagement produces a different — often deeper — reading experience.

body.speaker: Student
body.text: There's another problem I keep running into. My main character went through experiences I haven't had — she grew up in poverty in a country I've never visited. I worry I'm either getting it wrong or appropriating something I have no right to.

body.speaker: Tutor
body.text: That's one of the most important ethical questions in contemporary fiction, and I don't think there's a clean answer. The argument for restraint is real: writing from within an experience you don't share risks producing a distorted or exploitative representation. The argument for imaginative reach is equally real: if writers only portray lives identical to their own, fiction collapses into memoir and loses its capacity to generate solidarity across difference.

body.speaker: Student
body.text: So how do you navigate it?

body.speaker: Tutor
body.text: Preparation, humility, and formal choices. Research deeply — not just facts, but texture, register, the specific sensory world of the experience you're representing. Then make structural choices that acknowledge the limits of your access. If your narrator doesn't share that background, the gaps in their understanding can be made visible rather than papered over.

body.speaker: Student
body.text: That actually connects to something else I'm struggling with. I've been told to 'show, don't tell', but I'm not always sure what that means at the sentence level. How do you know when you're telling rather than showing?

body.speaker: Tutor
body.text: A useful test: ask whether you're naming an abstraction or embodying it. 'She was angry' tells. 'She set the cup down as if it had offended her' shows. The second sentence lets the reader infer the emotional state from the physical action — it invites interpretation rather than foreclosing it.

body.speaker: Student
body.text: But isn't there a risk that showing becomes its own kind of cliché — the aggressive cup-setting, the clenched jaw, the hollow eyes?

body.speaker: Tutor
body.text: Absolutely — and that's the real craft challenge. The instruction to 'show, don't tell' is a useful corrective for beginning writers, but it shouldn't become a dogma. Sometimes telling is exactly what a passage needs — when you want to establish emotional distance, create irony, or move through time rapidly. The question is always: what does this specific moment of the story require?

body.speaker: Student
body.text: I think I've been so anxious about the rules that I've lost track of what I'm actually trying to say. What's the most important thing to hold onto when you're deep in a draft and everything feels like it's coming apart?

body.speaker: Tutor
body.text: The question your story is asking. Every piece of fiction worth reading is animated by a question it doesn't fully answer — something it turns over and examines from multiple angles without resolving. If you know what question your story is asking, that question becomes your compass. Every scene, every sentence should either advance toward that question or earn its distance from it.

body.speaker: Student
body.text: What if I don't know the question yet?

body.speaker: Tutor
body.text: Then finishing the draft is how you find it. Don't expect the first draft to know what it's about. It's a process of discovery — you write your way toward the question, and the question only becomes clear in retrospect. That's why revision is where writing really happens.

annotations.meaningVi: giọng kể chuyện — cách thức và quan điểm mà câu chuyện được kể
annotations.exampleEn: The choice of narrative voice determines how much the reader can trust the narrator.

annotations.meaningVi: người kể chuyện toàn tri ngôi thứ ba — biết tất cả về mọi nhân vật
annotations.exampleEn: Tolstoy employed a third-person omniscient narrator to move fluidly between battlefields and drawing rooms.

annotations.meaningVi: trọng lượng tư tưởng — ý nghĩa và giả định chính trị tiềm ẩn trong một lựa chọn
annotations.exampleEn: Every narrative choice carries ideological weight whether or not the author intends it.

annotations.meaningVi: ngôi thứ ba gần — kỹ thuật kể chuyện ở ngôi thứ ba nhưng bám sát ý thức một nhân vật
annotations.exampleEn: Close third-person gives the intimacy of first-person without limiting the narrator's flexibility.

annotations.meaningVi: thế giới nội tâm — ý thức, cảm xúc và suy nghĩ bên trong của nhân vật
annotations.exampleEn: The novel's power comes from its relentless focus on the protagonist's interiority.

annotations.meaningVi: ý thức trung tâm — thuật ngữ của Henry James về nhân vật lọc kinh nghiệm trong tiểu thuyết
annotations.exampleEn: James placed the central consciousness at the heart of his late novels to intensify dramatic tension.

annotations.meaningVi: sự hạn chế tạo ra áp lực — ý tưởng rằng giới hạn góc nhìn tăng cường kịch tính
annotations.exampleEn: The unreliable narrator proves that limitation generates pressure in fiction.

annotations.meaningVi: chiếm đoạt văn hóa — việc sử dụng các yếu tố của nền văn hóa khác theo cách bị coi là thiếu tôn trọng hoặc khai thác
annotations.exampleEn: The novel sparked accusations of cultural appropriation because the author had no lived connection to the community she depicted.

annotations.meaningVi: tầm với của trí tưởng tượng — khả năng tưởng tượng và thể hiện trải nghiệm khác với trải nghiệm của mình
annotations.exampleEn: Great fiction requires imaginative reach into lives the author has never lived.

annotations.meaningVi: tình liên đới vượt qua sự khác biệt — sự đồng cảm và hiểu biết giữa những người có hoàn cảnh khác nhau
annotations.exampleEn: Literature's capacity to generate solidarity across difference is one of its most powerful social functions.

annotations.meaningVi: che đậy, che giấu vấn đề bề mặt thay vì giải quyết
annotations.exampleEn: The compromise agreement papered over the fundamental disagreements within the coalition.

annotations.meaningVi: thể hiện, đừng kể — nguyên tắc viết văn yêu cầu thể hiện cảm xúc qua hành động thay vì nêu thẳng
annotations.exampleEn: 'Show, don't tell' is one of the first principles taught in creative writing workshops.

annotations.meaningVi: hiện thân hóa một khái niệm trừu tượng — biến ý tưởng thành hình ảnh cụ thể
annotations.exampleEn: Good metaphors work by embodying an abstraction in a concrete image the reader can visualise.

annotations.meaningVi: đóng khả năng diễn giải — ngăn người đọc tự suy luận
annotations.exampleEn: Naming the emotion directly risks foreclosing interpretation and reducing reader engagement.

annotations.meaningVi: sự mỉa mai — kỹ thuật văn học khi ý nghĩa thực khác với những gì được nói
annotations.exampleEn: The narrative irony emerges from the gap between what the character believes and what the reader knows.

annotations.meaningVi: được thúc đẩy bởi một câu hỏi — khi một tác phẩm có câu hỏi trung tâm thay vì câu trả lời
annotations.exampleEn: The best novels are animated by a question they circle without ever fully resolving.

annotations.meaningVi: nhìn lại, sau khi sự việc đã xảy ra
annotations.exampleEn: Only in retrospect did the significance of that early scene become apparent.

annotations.meaningVi: khoảng cách tự sự — mức độ gần gũi hoặc xa cách giữa người kể chuyện và nhân vật hoặc sự kiện
annotations.exampleEn: Shifting narrative distance between scenes can modulate the reader's emotional engagement.

annotations.meaningVi: quá trình khám phá — viết như cách tìm hiểu, không biết trước kết quả
annotations.exampleEn: The first draft is a process of discovery — what you find changes what you keep.

annotations.meaningVi: lựa chọn dự phòng, lựa chọn mặc định khi không suy nghĩ kỹ
annotations.exampleEn: Using clichéd imagery as a fallback signals that the writer hasn't found their own language yet.

annotations.meaningVi: có ý nghĩa về mặt cấu trúc — đóng vai trò thiết yếu trong cách tác phẩm hoạt động
annotations.exampleEn: The gaps in the narrator's knowledge are structurally significant to how the mystery unfolds.

grammarNotes.title: Conditional inversion and hypothetical register in literary discussion
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong văn phong thảo luận về văn học, giả thiết thường được thể hiện qua đảo ngữ. 'If writers only portray lives identical to their own, fiction collapses into memoir' là điều kiện loại 1 có tính tổng quát. Trong văn nói học thuật, các cấu trúc 'Should you choose X…', 'Were this to happen…', 'Had the author…' xuất hiện thường xuyên để diễn đạt điều kiện phản thực hoặc khuyến nghị. Khi tutor nói 'Consider close third-person', đây là dạng mệnh lệnh mềm — dùng 'consider' thay vì 'use' để thể hiện gợi ý hơn là chỉ thị.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: In academic and workshop dialogue, imperatives are softened to suggestions: 'Consider close third-person' rather than 'Use close third-person'. The modal distance ('might', 'could', 'can work') performs epistemic humility appropriate to aesthetic advice, where there is no single correct answer. Notice also 'That can work, but it needs to be a deliberate choice' — this construction (acknowledge + condition) is the workshop register for qualified approval. Learning to use softened imperatives and modal hedging is essential for C1-level academic and professional discourse.

grammarNotes.title: Parallel structure and the rhetorical list in argumentative dialogue
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cấu trúc song song (parallel structure) tạo ra nhịp điệu hùng biện trong tranh luận. Ví dụ: 'The argument for restraint is real: writing from within an experience you don't share risks… The argument for imaginative reach is equally real: if writers only portray…' — hai mệnh đề song song 'The argument for X is real' + 'The argument for Y is equally real' tạo ra sự đối xứng rõ ràng và cho thấy người nói đang cân nhắc cả hai phía một cách công bằng. Cấu trúc này phổ biến trong phong cách hùng biện học thuật khi thảo luận về những vấn đề có hai mặt.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Parallel construction is a key rhetorical device in academic dialogue: 'The argument for restraint is real… The argument for imaginative reach is equally real.' The repetition of 'The argument for X is real' signals the speaker is presenting both sides with equal weight before adjudicating between them. The rhetorical list in the tutor's final answer — 'Research deeply… Then make structural choices… If your narrator doesn't share that background…' — creates a procedural rhythm that makes abstract advice feel actionable. Parallel structure is both a clarity tool and a persuasive one.

translationVi:
Gia sư: Tôi đã đọc bản thảo mới của bạn, và có điều gì đó thực sự đầy hứa hẹn trong đó. Nhưng tôi muốn phản đối một lựa chọn bạn đã thực hiện gần như vô hình — giọng kể chuyện. Ai đang kể câu chuyện này, và họ biết bao nhiêu?

Sinh viên: Tôi giả định là người kể chuyện toàn tri ngôi thứ ba — người có thể nhìn thấy mọi thứ, biết suy nghĩ của mọi người. Đó không phải là... mặc định sao?

Gia sư: Đó chính xác là giả định tôi muốn thách thức. Toàn tri không phải là mặc định trung lập — đó là một lập trường mang trọng lượng tư tưởng riêng của nó. Khi bạn biết tất cả mọi thứ về mọi nhân vật cùng một lúc, bạn đang thực hiện một loại quyền uy như Chúa trời đối với họ. Điều đó có thể hiệu quả, nhưng nó cần là một lựa chọn có chủ đích, không phải là lựa chọn dự phòng.

Sinh viên: Vậy thì giải pháp thay thế là gì? Tôi không muốn bị mắc kẹt trong đầu một nhân vật suốt.

Gia sư: Bạn không cần phải vậy. Hãy xem xét ngôi thứ ba gần — về mặt kỹ thuật bạn ở bên ngoài nhân vật, trong ngữ pháp ngôi thứ ba, nhưng khả năng tiếp cận thế giới nội tâm của họ thì sâu sắc và có chọn lọc. Sự hạn chế chính là điểm mấu chốt: những gì người kể chuyện không biết tạo ra ý nghĩa nhiều như những gì họ biết.

Sinh viên: Điều đó gợi nhớ cho tôi điều Henry James viết về 'ý thức trung tâm' — ý tưởng rằng lọc kinh nghiệm qua một góc nhìn hạn chế thực sự tạo ra cường độ nhiều hơn là quyền tiếp cận toàn bộ.

Gia sư: Chính xác. James hiểu rằng sự hạn chế tạo ra áp lực. Khi bạn chỉ có thể nhận thức qua một ý thức, những khoảng trống và sự không chắc chắn trở nên có ý nghĩa về mặt cấu trúc. Người đọc thực hiện công việc diễn giải mà người kể chuyện toàn tri nếu không thì sẽ làm cho họ, và sự tham gia đó tạo ra một trải nghiệm đọc khác — thường sâu sắc hơn.

Sinh viên: Còn có một vấn đề khác tôi cứ gặp phải. Nhân vật chính của tôi đã trải qua những kinh nghiệm tôi chưa có — cô ấy lớn lên trong nghèo đói ở một đất nước tôi chưa bao giờ đến thăm. Tôi lo lắng mình có thể làm sai hoặc chiếm đoạt thứ gì đó mà tôi không có quyền.

Gia sư: Đó là một trong những câu hỏi đạo đức quan trọng nhất trong văn học đương đại, và tôi không nghĩ có câu trả lời rõ ràng. Lập luận cho sự kiềm chế là có thực: viết từ bên trong một trải nghiệm bạn không chia sẻ có nguy cơ tạo ra một hình ảnh đại diện bị bóp méo hoặc có tính khai thác. Lập luận cho tầm với của trí tưởng tượng cũng có thực như vậy: nếu các nhà văn chỉ mô tả những cuộc sống giống hệt cuộc sống của họ, văn học hư cấu sẽ sụp đổ thành hồi ký và mất đi khả năng tạo ra tình liên đới vượt qua sự khác biệt.

Sinh viên: Vậy làm thế nào để định hướng điều đó?

Gia sư: Sự chuẩn bị, sự khiêm tốn, và các lựa chọn về hình thức. Nghiên cứu sâu — không chỉ các sự kiện, mà còn kết cấu, ngữ điệu, thế giới cảm giác cụ thể của trải nghiệm bạn đang đại diện. Sau đó đưa ra các lựa chọn có tính cấu trúc thừa nhận giới hạn tiếp cận của bạn. Nếu người kể chuyện của bạn không chia sẻ bối cảnh đó, những khoảng trống trong sự hiểu biết của họ có thể được làm hiện rõ thay vì được che đậy.

Sinh viên: Điều đó thực sự kết nối với điều khác tôi đang vật lộn. Tôi đã được nói 'thể hiện, đừng kể', nhưng tôi không phải lúc nào cũng chắc điều đó có nghĩa là gì ở cấp độ câu. Làm thế nào để biết khi nào bạn đang kể thay vì thể hiện?

Gia sư: Một bài kiểm tra hữu ích: hỏi xem bạn có đang đặt tên cho một sự trừu tượng hay thể hiện nó không. 'Cô ấy tức giận' là kể. 'Cô ấy đặt cốc xuống như thể nó đã xúc phạm cô ấy' là thể hiện. Câu thứ hai để người đọc suy luận trạng thái cảm xúc từ hành động vật lý — nó mời sự diễn giải thay vì đóng khả năng diễn giải lại.

Sinh viên: Nhưng chẳng phải có nguy cơ rằng thể hiện trở thành sáo ngữ của chính nó — cái đặt cốc hung hăng, cái hàm siết chặt, cái mắt trống rỗng?

Gia sư: Hoàn toàn đúng — và đó là thách thức nghệ thuật thực sự. Chỉ dẫn 'thể hiện, đừng kể' là một sự điều chỉnh hữu ích cho những người viết mới bắt đầu, nhưng nó không nên trở thành giáo điều. Đôi khi kể chính xác là những gì một đoạn văn cần — khi bạn muốn tạo ra khoảng cách cảm xúc, tạo ra sự mỉa mai, hoặc di chuyển qua thời gian một cách nhanh chóng. Câu hỏi luôn là: khoảnh khắc cụ thể này của câu chuyện cần gì?

Sinh viên: Tôi nghĩ tôi đã lo lắng về các quy tắc đến mức không còn theo dõi những gì tôi thực sự đang cố nói. Điều quan trọng nhất để nắm giữ khi bạn đang viết sâu vào một bản thảo và mọi thứ có cảm giác đang tan rã là gì?

Gia sư: Câu hỏi mà câu chuyện của bạn đang đặt ra. Mỗi tác phẩm văn học hư cấu đáng đọc đều được thúc đẩy bởi một câu hỏi mà nó không trả lời đầy đủ — thứ gì đó nó lật đi lật lại và xem xét từ nhiều góc độ mà không giải quyết. Nếu bạn biết câu hỏi mà câu chuyện của bạn đang đặt ra, câu hỏi đó trở thành la bàn của bạn. Mỗi cảnh, mỗi câu nên tiến về phía câu hỏi đó hoặc xứng đáng với khoảng cách của nó.

Sinh viên: Nếu tôi chưa biết câu hỏi thì sao?

Gia sư: Thì hoàn thành bản thảo là cách bạn tìm ra nó. Đừng kỳ vọng bản thảo đầu tiên biết nó đang nói về gì. Đó là quá trình khám phá — bạn viết để tiến đến câu hỏi, và câu hỏi chỉ trở nên rõ ràng khi nhìn lại. Đó là lý do tại sao việc sửa bản là nơi viết thực sự xảy ra.

questions.prompt: Why does the tutor challenge the student's use of a third-person omniscient narrator?
options: Because omniscient narration is technically difficult to sustain across a long novel
Because omniscience carries ideological weight — it enacts god-like authority over characters and must be a deliberate choice
Because omniscient narrators are considered old-fashioned in contemporary literary fiction
Because the student lacks the writing experience to handle multiple characters' perspectives simultaneously
questions.explanation: The tutor says omniscience 'is not a neutral default — it's a stance that carries its own ideological weight' and must be 'a deliberate choice, not a fallback'.
questions.hint: The key term is 'ideological weight' — what does the tutor mean by this?

questions.prompt: What is the key feature of 'close third-person' narration as described by the tutor?
options: It uses first-person pronouns while describing external events
It uses third-person grammar but provides deep, selective access to one character's interiority
It switches between multiple characters' perspectives within a single scene
It maintains complete neutrality by refusing to access any character's inner life
questions.explanation: The tutor describes close third-person as 'technically outside the character, in third-person grammar, but your access to their interiority is deep and selective'.
questions.hint: What combination of grammar and access defines this technique?

questions.prompt: What does the tutor mean when she says 'limitation generates pressure'?
options: That writing under deadlines produces better prose than writing at leisure
That restricting access to information creates structural meaning and requires readers to do interpretive work
That characters under psychological pressure make more interesting protagonists
That the physical constraints of a limited setting intensify dramatic conflict
questions.explanation: The tutor explains that with a limited perspective, 'gaps and uncertainties become structurally significant' and 'the reader does interpretive work the omniscient narrator would otherwise do for them'.
questions.hint: Think about what 'pressure' means in terms of reader experience and narrative gaps.

questions.prompt: How does the tutor characterise the ethical dilemma of writing characters whose experiences the author hasn't shared?
options: As a problem with a clear answer: authors should only write from their own experience
As a false dilemma that can be resolved through sufficient research
As a genuine tension between the real risks of distorted representation and the real value of imaginative reach
As primarily a legal issue relating to intellectual property and cultural ownership
questions.explanation: The tutor says 'I don't think there's a clean answer' and presents 'the argument for restraint' and 'the argument for imaginative reach' as 'equally real'.
questions.hint: Notice the tutor's exact words: 'The argument for X is real… The argument for Y is equally real.'

questions.prompt: What specific advice does the tutor give for writing characters from unfamiliar backgrounds?
options: Avoid writing such characters altogether and stay within familiar experience
Use a frame narrator who is explicitly an outsider to the community being depicted
Research deeply for texture and register, then make structural choices that acknowledge the limits of your access
Consult with members of the community and obtain their explicit permission before publishing
questions.explanation: The tutor advises: 'Research deeply — not just facts, but texture, register, the specific sensory world… Then make structural choices that acknowledge the limits of your access.'
questions.hint: The tutor gives a three-part answer: preparation, humility, and formal choices.

questions.prompt: What is the tutor's test for distinguishing 'showing' from 'telling'?
options: Telling uses short sentences; showing uses longer, more detailed descriptions
Telling names an abstraction directly; showing embodies it in a physical or specific detail that invites inference
Telling uses adjectives; showing uses verbs and action sequences
Telling is appropriate for minor characters; showing should be reserved for the protagonist
questions.explanation: The tutor says: 'ask whether you're naming an abstraction or embodying it. \"She was angry\" tells. \"She set the cup down as if it had offended her\" shows.'
questions.hint: The key contrast is between 'naming an abstraction' and 'embodying it'.

questions.prompt: How does the tutor qualify the 'show, don't tell' principle?
options: She argues it only applies to literary fiction and not genre writing
She says it is a useful corrective for beginners but should not become a dogma — telling is sometimes exactly what a passage needs
She reverses her position, arguing that telling is almost always superior to showing
She says the principle only applies to scenes involving strong emotion, not to plot exposition
questions.explanation: The tutor says the principle 'shouldn't become a dogma' and that 'sometimes telling is exactly what a passage needs — when you want to establish emotional distance, create irony, or move through time rapidly'.
questions.hint: The tutor uses the word 'dogma' to signal a qualification.

questions.prompt: What does the tutor describe as the 'compass' a writer should hold onto during a difficult draft?
options: The character arc of the protagonist
The emotional tone established in the opening scene
The question the story is asking — something it examines without fully resolving
The genre conventions and structural expectations of the form
questions.explanation: The tutor says 'The question your story is asking' is the compass: 'Every piece of fiction worth reading is animated by a question it doesn't fully answer.'
questions.hint: The tutor uses the word 'compass' explicitly — what does she say serves as this compass?

questions.prompt: What does the tutor mean by saying 'revision is where writing really happens'?
options: That the first draft should be written very slowly and carefully to avoid errors
That editing for grammar and style is the most important stage of the writing process
That the first draft is discovery — the real meaning and question of a piece only becomes clear through subsequent revision
That writers should always collaborate with an editor rather than revising independently
questions.explanation: The tutor says: 'Don't expect the first draft to know what it's about. It's a process of discovery — you write your way toward the question, and the question only becomes clear in retrospect.'
questions.hint: Connect 'process of discovery' with 'in retrospect' to understand what revision involves.

questions.prompt: What risk does the student identify with 'showing' rather than 'telling'?
options: That it makes the prose too slow and prevents the story from moving forward
That showing becomes its own kind of cliché — using stock physical gestures like aggressive cup-setting or clenched jaws
That readers cannot be trusted to infer emotional states from physical actions
That showing requires more words and inflates the length of the manuscript unnecessarily
questions.explanation: The student asks: 'isn't there a risk that showing becomes its own kind of cliché — the aggressive cup-setting, the clenched jaw, the hollow eyes?'
questions.hint: The student lists specific examples of what she fears showing could become.

questions.prompt: What does Henry James's concept of the 'central consciousness' have in common with the tutor's concept of 'close third-person'?
options: Both require a first-person narrator who reflects directly on their own unreliability
Both argue that filtering experience through a limited perspective creates more intensity than total access
Both were developed as responses to the traditions of Victorian omniscient narration
Both require the narrator to be a morally superior observer of the events described
questions.explanation: The student connects them: 'filtering experience through a limited perspective actually creates more intensity than total access would'. The tutor agrees: 'James understood that limitation generates pressure.'
questions.hint: What did James and the tutor's description have in common about the effect of limitation?

questions.prompt: What does 'papered over' mean in the context of the tutor's advice about writing unfamiliar backgrounds?
options: Documented carefully in footnotes or author's notes
Hidden by covering the gaps in understanding with superficial detail rather than acknowledging them structurally
Removed from the manuscript before publication to avoid controversy
Presented as if they were the author's own lived experience
questions.explanation: The tutor says 'the gaps in their understanding can be made visible rather than papered over' — meaning writers should acknowledge limits rather than conceal them with superficial writing.
questions.hint: 'Papered over' is an idiom meaning to conceal a problem by covering its surface.

questions.prompt: Why does the tutor describe omniscient narration as enacting 'god-like authority'?
options: Because it requires the author to invent events that could not be directly observed by any character
Because knowing everything about every character simultaneously places the narrator above all human limitations of perspective
Because omniscient narrators typically pass moral judgements on the characters they describe
Because readers associate omniscient narration with religious texts and therefore grant it unquestioned authority
questions.explanation: God-like authority refers to the omniscient narrator's position above the story, able to access all perspectives simultaneously — a position no human occupant of the story actually has.
questions.hint: What is uniquely 'god-like' about knowing everything about everyone at once?

questions.prompt: The tutor says fiction can 'generate solidarity across difference'. What does this mean in the context of the debate about writing unfamiliar experiences?
options: That fictional characters from different backgrounds should always befriend each other by the end of the story
That literature can create empathetic understanding between readers and people whose lives they don't share — a value that justifies imaginative reach
That the profits from fictional works should be shared with the communities they depict
That solidarity between writers of different backgrounds produces better collective fiction than individual authorship
questions.explanation: The tutor argues that if fiction only depicts the author's own experience, it 'loses its capacity to generate solidarity across difference' — meaning literature can foster empathy for lives the reader doesn't know.
questions.hint: Why is solidarity across difference valuable, and how does fiction uniquely generate it?

questions.prompt: What overall view of creative rules does the dialogue as a whole convey?
options: Rules in creative writing are arbitrary conventions that should be ignored in favour of instinct
Rules are absolute laws that must be followed to produce publishable work
Rules are useful starting points and correctives but must be applied with judgement to the specific demands of each moment
Rules should be learned and then deliberately broken as a sign of artistic maturity
questions.explanation: The tutor consistently qualifies rules: omniscience 'can work' if deliberate; 'show, don't tell' 'shouldn't become a dogma'; telling 'is sometimes exactly what a passage needs'. The pattern is: rule + context-dependent qualification.
questions.hint: Track the tutor's approach to every rule mentioned — does she endorse it absolutely or qualify it?

cloze.template: The tutor challenges the student's use of third-person {{b1}} narration, arguing it is 'not a neutral default' but carries ideological weight. An alternative is {{b2}} third-person, which maintains third-person grammar while providing deep, selective access to a character's {{b3}}. The student references Henry James's concept of the '{{b4}}', noting that filtering experience through a limited perspective generates more {{b5}} than total access. The tutor agrees: limitation generates {{b6}}, and gaps in the narrator's knowledge become {{b7}} significant. On the ethics of writing characters whose experiences you haven't shared, the tutor argues there is no clean answer: writing from within unfamiliar experience risks {{b8}} representation, while restricting writers to their own experience destroys fiction's capacity to generate {{b9}} across difference. Her advice is to research not just facts but {{b10}}, register, and the specific sensory world of the experience, then make structural choices that acknowledge limits rather than {{b11}} them. The principle of 'show, don't tell' means embodying an abstraction rather than {{b12}} it — 'She set the cup down as if it had offended her' shows because it lets the reader {{b13}} the emotion. However, showing can become a {{b14}} in its own right if writers rely on stock gestures. Sometimes {{b15}} is exactly what a passage needs — to establish emotional distance, create irony, or move rapidly through time. Finally, every piece of fiction worth reading is {{b16}} by a question it doesn't fully answer, and knowing that question provides a compass when the draft feels chaotic.

blanks.explanation: The student describes using a 'third-person omniscient narrator'.
The tutor proposes 'close third-person' as the alternative.
The tutor says close third-person gives 'deep and selective' access to a character's 'interiority'.
The student references Henry James's concept of the 'central consciousness'.
The student says limited perspective 'creates more intensity than total access would'.
The tutor says gaps and uncertainties 'become structurally significant'.
The tutor says writing from unfamiliar experience 'risks producing a distorted or exploitative representation'.
The tutor refers to fiction's capacity to 'generate solidarity across difference'.
The tutor advises researching 'not just facts, but texture, register, the specific sensory world'.
The tutor says 'gaps in their understanding can be made visible rather than papered over'.
The tutor contrasts 'embodying' an abstraction with 'naming' it — 'She was angry' names the emotion.
The showing technique 'lets the reader infer the emotional state from the physical action'.
The student asks whether showing can 'become its own kind of cliché'.
The tutor says 'sometimes telling is exactly what a passage needs'.
The tutor says 'Every piece of fiction worth reading is animated by a question it doesn't fully answer'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The tutor argues that fiction loses its capacity to 'generate solidarity across difference' if writers are restricted to depicting only their own lived experience. But critics of cultural appropriation argue that this defence has historically been used to justify distorted representations that cause real harm to marginalised communities. Is there a principled way to distinguish legitimate imaginative reach from exploitative appropriation, or is this distinction inherently contested and politically determined? Who should have the authority to draw this line?

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: I've read your new draft, and there's something genuinely promising in it.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: But I want to push back on a choice you've made almost invisibly — the narrative voice.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: Who is telling this story, and how much do they know?

sentences.speaker: Student
sentences.text: I assumed a third-person omniscient narrator — someone who can see everything, knows what everyone's thinking.

sentences.speaker: Student
sentences.text: Isn't that just… the default?

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: That's exactly the assumption I'd challenge.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: Omniscience is not a neutral default — it's a stance that carries its own ideological weight.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: When you know everything about every character simultaneously, you're enacting a kind of god-like authority over them.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: That can work, but it needs to be a deliberate choice, not a fallback.

sentences.speaker: Student
sentences.text: So what's the alternative?

sentences.speaker: Student
sentences.text: I don't want to be trapped inside one character's head the whole time.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: You don't have to be.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: Consider close third-person — you're technically outside the character, in third-person grammar, but your access to their interiority is deep and selective.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: The restriction is the point: what the narrator doesn't know creates as much meaning as what they do.

sentences.speaker: Student
sentences.text: That reminds me of something Henry James wrote about the 'central consciousness' — the idea that filtering experience through a limited perspective actually creates more intensity than total access would.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: Precisely.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: James understood that limitation generates pressure.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: When you can only perceive through one consciousness, the gaps and uncertainties become structurally significant.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: The reader does interpretive work the omniscient narrator would otherwise do for them, and that engagement produces a different — often deeper — reading experience.

sentences.speaker: Student
sentences.text: There's another problem I keep running into.

sentences.speaker: Student
sentences.text: My main character went through experiences I haven't had — she grew up in poverty in a country I've never visited.

sentences.speaker: Student
sentences.text: I worry I'm either getting it wrong or appropriating something I have no right to.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: That's one of the most important ethical questions in contemporary fiction, and I don't think there's a clean answer.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: The argument for restraint is real: writing from within an experience you don't share risks producing a distorted or exploitative representation.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: The argument for imaginative reach is equally real: if writers only portray lives identical to their own, fiction collapses into memoir and loses its capacity to generate solidarity across difference.

sentences.speaker: Student
sentences.text: So how do you navigate it?

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: Preparation, humility, and formal choices.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: Research deeply — not just facts, but texture, register, the specific sensory world of the experience you're representing.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: Then make structural choices that acknowledge the limits of your access.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: If your narrator doesn't share that background, the gaps in their understanding can be made visible rather than papered over.

sentences.speaker: Student
sentences.text: That actually connects to something else I'm struggling with.

sentences.speaker: Student
sentences.text: I've been told to 'show, don't tell', but I'm not always sure what that means at the sentence level.

sentences.speaker: Student
sentences.text: How do you know when you're telling rather than showing?

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: A useful test: ask whether you're naming an abstraction or embodying it.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: 'She was angry' tells.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: 'She set the cup down as if it had offended her' shows.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: The second sentence lets the reader infer the emotional state from the physical action — it invites interpretation rather than foreclosing it.

sentences.speaker: Student
sentences.text: But isn't there a risk that showing becomes its own kind of cliché — the aggressive cup-setting, the clenched jaw, the hollow eyes?

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: Absolutely — and that's the real craft challenge.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: The instruction to 'show, don't tell' is a useful corrective for beginning writers, but it shouldn't become a dogma.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: Sometimes telling is exactly what a passage needs — when you want to establish emotional distance, create irony, or move through time rapidly.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: The question is always: what does this specific moment of the story require?

sentences.speaker: Student
sentences.text: I think I've been so anxious about the rules that I've lost track of what I'm actually trying to say.

sentences.speaker: Student
sentences.text: What's the most important thing to hold onto when you're deep in a draft and everything feels like it's coming apart?

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: The question your story is asking.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: Every piece of fiction worth reading is animated by a question it doesn't fully answer — something it turns over and examines from multiple angles without resolving.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: If you know what question your story is asking, that question becomes your compass.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: Every scene, every sentence should either advance toward that question or earn its distance from it.

sentences.speaker: Student
sentences.text: What if I don't know the question yet?

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: Then finishing the draft is how you find it.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: Don't expect the first draft to know what it's about.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: It's a process of discovery — you write your way toward the question, and the question only becomes clear in retrospect.

sentences.speaker: Tutor
sentences.text: That's why revision is where writing really happens.
